feynman.0: For a hundred years, every interference experiment said the same thing: light is a wave. But there was one experiment that simply wouldn’t play along. It turns out light hands over its energy in little indivisible lumps we call photons — and the photoelectric effect is how we catch them in the act.
feynman.1: Light hands over its energy in indivisible lumps called photons. A photon of frequency f carries one fixed amount of energy, set by that frequency — it can’t hand over half a lump, and a surface can’t slowly soak it up bit by bit. Planck’s constant h is just the number that ties energy to frequency:
feynman.2: Measured in joules, photon energies come out as absurdly tiny numbers, so all through this area of study we work instead in electron-volts: the energy one electron picks up sliding through a potential difference of 1 V. Put q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C into W = qV and out drops the conversion:
feynman.3: The formula sheet hands you Planck’s constant in both flavours. Reach for h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s when you want the answer in electron-volts, and h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s when you want it in joules — pick the right one and you’ve quietly skipped a whole conversion step.
feynman.4: Anika aims a green laser pointer — wavelength 532 nm, output power 1.0 mW — at a screen. She wants to work out the energy of a single photon, in joules and in electron-volts, and how many photons go streaming out of the laser every second.
feynman.7: Substitution (electron-volts): E = (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 3.0 × 10⁸) ÷ (5.32 × 10⁻⁷) = 2.3 eV — the very same energy, since 3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹ ÷ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.3.
feynman.8: Answer: every photon carries 2.3 eV. The laser pours out 1.0 × 10⁻³ J of energy each second, so the number of photons it throws off per second is n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵.
feynman.9: Key idea Frequency alone fixes how much energy a photon carries: E = hf. Intensity only sets how many photons show up each second — it never touches how much each one is carrying.
feynman.10: The photoelectric effect is just this: shine light of high-enough frequency on a metal and electrons come flying off. The gadget in Figure 8.1 is how we measure it — single-colour light goes into an evacuated tube and lands on a metal plate, the knocked-out photoelectrons sail across the gap to a collector electrode, and an ammeter reads off the photocurrent they make. A variable DC supply sets the voltage between the two electrodes, and you can flip its polarity whenever you like.
feynman.11: Make the collector positive and it reels in every electron that comes off; the photocurrent saturates: crank the collector more positive than that and you get not one bit of extra current, because you’re already catching every photoelectron there is. Now make the collector negative instead, and each electron crossing the gap has to spend kinetic energy W = qV fighting the field pushing back at it — so as you turn up the reverse voltage, the sluggish electrons are the first to get turned around, and the current drops.
feynman.12: Turn the reverse voltage up to the stopping voltage V0 and even the fastest photoelectrons run out of speed before they reach the collector, so the photocurrent falls all the way to zero. That’s what makes the stopping voltage so handy — it’s a direct measure of the maximum kinetic energy the photoelectrons had:
feynman.13: Do it in electron-volts and the conversion just melts away: a stopping voltage of 1.40 V means straight off that Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Figure 8.2 lays out the whole photocurrent–voltage curve for the same light at two different intensities.
feynman.14: Einstein made sense of all this with the photon picture. Each photon gets swallowed whole by a single electron — no electron grabs two of them — and the most loosely held electrons need some minimum energy φ, the work function of that particular metal, just to claw their way out of the surface. An electron right at the surface gets to keep whatever’s left over:
feynman.15: Electrons that start out deeper inside the metal lose a bit more energy clawing their way to the surface, so they come out with less than Ek max. Set Ek max = 0 and you find the threshold frequency f0 — the lowest frequency that can pry even a single electron loose from that metal:
feynman.16: Plot Ek max against f and Einstein’s equation turns into a dead-straight line, the one in Figure 8.3. Just hold Ek max = hf − φ up next to y = mx + c and the graph reads itself: the slope is h — the same for every metal there is — the spot where it crosses the horizontal axis is f0, and run the line back and it hits the vertical axis at −φ. Swap in a different metal and you get a parallel line: both intercepts shift, but the slope never budges.
feynman.17: In the photon picture, the intensity of single-colour light is nothing but the number of photons turning up each second, each one carrying its hf. Hold the frequency fixed somewhere above f0 and double the intensity: now you’ve doubled the photoelectrons kicked out per second, so the saturation current doubles too — but each electron is still being knocked out by one photon of the same energy as before, so Ek max and V0 don’t move at all, exactly as Figure 8.2 shows. Raise the frequency instead while keeping the light power the same, and each photon now carries more energy, so fewer of them arrive each second — and the saturation current drops.
feynman.18: Trap Brighter never means faster — don’t let yourself believe it does. Turning up the intensity only changes the photocurrent; Ek max and the stopping voltage are set by the frequency and the work function, full stop. And light below the threshold frequency kicks out nothing whatsoever, no matter how bright you make it or how long you leave it shining.
feynman.19: Kenji shines violet light of frequency 7.5 × 10¹⁴ Hz onto the caesium surface of a photocell. Caesium’s work function is φ = 2.1 eV.
feynman.20: Formula: Ek max = hf − φ, with h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s, since the data come to us in electron-volts.
feynman.22: Answer: Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, so the stopping voltage works out to V0 = 1.0 V. The threshold frequency of caesium is f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz, so this violet light sits comfortably above the threshold.
feynman.23: Connects Measuring the stopping voltage is really just W = qV from electric fields run backwards — here the field strips kinetic energy away instead of pumping it in — and the electron-volt we defined back there is the working currency of this whole area of study.
feynman.24: By 1905 the wave model of light had a hundred years of interference evidence stacked behind it. Point it at the photoelectric effect, though, and it makes three perfectly clear predictions — and every single one of them turns out to be wrong.
feynman.25: The photon picture clears up all three at once. Energy shows up in indivisible lumps of hf, one photon knocks out one electron, and the handover is instant: if hf < φ the electron just can’t get out and there’s nothing to slowly build up, while if hf > φ the very first photons that land start kicking electrons loose with no delay at all. Frequency sets how much energy each lump carries; intensity sets only how many lumps turn up each second.
feynman.26: Key idea The photoelectric effect is the evidence that light comes in quanta. The wave model trips over all three — intensity, threshold frequency and time delay — while the photon model nails every one with E = hf.
feynman.27: Connects Here’s a model running headlong into evidence it can’t explain away; the mirror-image case is waiting on the next page, where electron diffraction flatly contradicts the particle model of matter.

sagan.0: For a hundred years the patient evidence of interference had spoken with one voice, and that voice said: light is a wave. Yet a single stubborn experiment would not join the chorus. Light, it turned out, surrenders its energy not in a smooth and endless stream but in indivisible packets we call photons — and it is the photoelectric effect that lets us count them, one by one.
sagan.1: Light hands over its energy in indivisible packets called photons. A photon of frequency f carries one fixed measure of energy, exactly proportional to that frequency — it cannot offer half of itself, and no surface can quietly hoard it grain by grain until enough has gathered. The bridge between frequency and energy is a number of nature, Planck’s constant h:
sagan.2: Measured in joules, the energy of a single photon is so vanishingly small that the numbers grow unwieldy, so throughout this area of study we turn instead to the electron-volt: the energy one electron gathers as it falls through a potential difference of 1 V. Apply W = qV with q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C and the conversion reveals itself:
sagan.3: The formula sheet offers Planck’s constant in two currencies at once. Reach for h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s when you want the energy in electron-volts, and for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s when you want it in joules — choose wisely and an entire conversion step simply melts away.
sagan.4: Anika aims a green laser pointer, its wavelength 532 nm and its output power 1.0 mW, at a screen. She sets out to find the energy carried by each photon, in joules and in electron-volts, and then how many photons stream out of the laser in a single second.
sagan.7: Substitution (electron-volts): E = (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 3.0 × 10⁸) ÷ (5.32 × 10⁻⁷) = 2.3 eV — the very same energy, only spoken in a different tongue, since 3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹ ÷ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.3.
sagan.8: Answer: each photon carries 2.3 eV. The laser pours out 1.0 × 10⁻³ J of energy every second, so the multitude of photons it emits per second is n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵.
sagan.9: Key idea The energy of each photon is fixed by frequency, and by frequency alone: E = hf. Intensity decides only how many photons arrive each second — never how much energy any single one of them brings.
sagan.10: The photoelectric effect is the casting-out of electrons from a metal surface bathed in light of high enough frequency. The apparatus drawn in Figure 8.1 is built to measure it: monochromatic light enters an evacuated tube and falls upon a metal plate, the liberated photoelectrons leap across the gap to a collector electrode, and the photocurrent they make is read off an ammeter. A variable DC supply sets the potential difference between the electrodes, and at the turn of a switch its polarity can be reversed.
sagan.11: Make the collector positive and every ejected electron is drawn faithfully across the gap, until the photocurrent saturates: push the collector more positive still and not one extra electron answers, for every photoelectron is already being gathered in. Make the collector negative instead, and now each electron crossing the gap must spend kinetic energy W = qV against the opposing field — so as the reverse voltage climbs, the slowest electrons are turned back first, and the current ebbs away.
sagan.12: At the stopping voltage V0 even the swiftest photoelectrons are halted and brought to rest before they ever reach the collector, and the photocurrent falls to zero. The stopping voltage is therefore a measuring stick for the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons:
sagan.13: Work in electron-volts and the conversion vanishes altogether: a stopping voltage of 1.40 V means Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Figure 8.2 lays out the full photocurrent–voltage curve for this same light at two different intensities.
sagan.14: Einstein made sense of all these measurements through the photon model. Each photon is swallowed whole by a single electron — no electron ever sips from two — and the most loosely held electrons demand a minimum toll of energy φ, the work function of that metal, simply to break free of the surface. Whatever energy remains, a surface electron keeps for itself:
sagan.15: Electrons torn from deeper within the metal squander further energy fighting their way out, so they emerge carrying less than Ek max. Set Ek max = 0 and you uncover the threshold frequency f0, the least frequency that can pry any electron at all from that metal:
sagan.16: Plot Ek max against f and Einstein’s equation unfolds into a clean straight line, the one drawn in Figure 8.3. Set Ek max = hf − φ beside y = mx + c and the graph confesses everything at a glance: the gradient is h — the same for every metal in the universe — the horizontal intercept is f0, and the line, run backwards, meets the vertical axis at −φ. Different metals trace out parallel lines: change the metal and you shift both intercepts, but the gradient never yields.
sagan.17: In the photon model the intensity of monochromatic light is nothing more than the number of photons arriving each second, every one of them bearing hf. Hold the frequency fixed above f0 and double the intensity, and you double the photoelectrons cast out each second, and so double the saturation current — yet each electron is still struck loose by a single photon of unchanged energy, so Ek max and V0 stand exactly where they were, just as Figure 8.2 reveals. Raise the frequency instead while holding the light’s power steady, and now each photon carries more energy, so fewer of them arrive each second, and the saturation current falls.
sagan.18: Trap Brighter never means faster. Alter the intensity and you alter the photocurrent and nothing else; Ek max and the stopping voltage answer only to the frequency and the work function. And light below the threshold frequency casts out nothing whatever, however blindingly intense it may be, and however patiently it goes on shining.
sagan.19: Kenji shines violet light, its frequency 7.5 × 10¹⁴ Hz, onto the caesium surface of a photocell. The work function of caesium is φ = 2.1 eV.
sagan.20: Formula: Ek max = hf − φ, with h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s because the data come to us in electron-volts.
sagan.22: Answer: Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, so the stopping voltage is V0 = 1.0 V. The threshold frequency of caesium is f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz, so the violet light rides comfortably above the threshold.
sagan.23: Connects The stopping-voltage measurement is nothing but W = qV from electric fields run in reverse — here the field strips kinetic energy away rather than bestowing it — and the electron-volt first defined there is the working currency of this entire area of study.
sagan.24: By 1905 the wave model of light stood upon a century of interference evidence, a proud and well-earned inheritance. Yet turn it loose on the photoelectric effect and it makes three plain predictions — and one by one, every single one of them fails.
sagan.25: The photon model accounts for all three observations in a single stroke. Energy arrives in indivisible packets of hf, one photon casts out one electron, and the exchange is instantaneous: if hf < φ the electron can never escape and there is simply nothing to accumulate, while if hf > φ the very first photons to be absorbed eject electrons without the slightest delay. Frequency rules the energy of each packet; intensity rules only how many packets come.
sagan.26: Key idea The photoelectric effect is the evidence that light is quantised. The wave model fails on intensity, on threshold frequency and on time delay; the photon model explains all three at once with E = hf.
sagan.27: Connects Here a model collides with evidence it cannot possibly account for; the mirror image of this story waits on the next page, where electron diffraction rises up to contradict the particle model of matter.

cox.10: The photoelectric effect is the ejection of electrons from a metal surface when it is lit by light of high enough frequency. The apparatus drawn in Figure 8.1 is how we watch it happen: monochromatic light enters an evacuated tube and lands on a metal plate, the ejected photoelectrons fly across the gap to a collector electrode, and the photocurrent they make is read off an ammeter. cox.12: At the stopping voltage V0 even the very fastest photoelectrons are brought gently to rest before they reach the collector, and the photocurrent drops to zero. So the stopping voltage is, in effect, a direct measure of the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons:

Each photon is swallowed whole by a single electron — no electron ever takes in two — and the most loosely held electrons need a certain minimum energy φ, the work function of that metal, just to break free of the surface. cox.16: Plot Ek max against f and Einstein’s equation becomes a beautifully straight line, the one drawn in Figure 8.3. Lay Ek max = hf − φ alongside y = mx + c and the graph reads itself: the gradient is h — the same for every metal there is — the horizontal intercept is f0, and the line, run backwards, meets the vertical axis at −φ. greene.0: For a hundred years, interference had told a single, consistent story: light is a wave. Then one stubborn experiment refused to fit. Light, it turned out, hands over its energy not in a smooth flow but in indivisible parcels we call photons — and the photoelectric effect is the instrument that lets us count them.
greene.1: Light delivers its energy in indivisible packets called photons. Here is the surprising part: a photon of frequency f carries exactly one quantity of energy, and that quantity is proportional to the frequency. It cannot hand over half of itself, and no surface can sip at it gradually, drop by drop — the energy arrives whole or not at all. The constant of proportionality that ties energy to frequency is Planck’s constant h:
greene.2: Measured in joules, a photon’s energy is an awkwardly tiny number, so throughout this area of study we reach instead for the electron-volt: the energy one electron gains as it is accelerated through a potential difference of 1 V. Apply W = qV with q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C and the conversion falls straight out:
greene.3: The formula sheet does you a quiet favour: it prints Planck’s constant in both currencies at once. Reach for h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s when you want the answer in electron-volts, and for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s when you want it in joules. Pick the right one at the start and an entire conversion step simply vanishes.
greene.4: Anika aims a green laser pointer — wavelength 532 nm, output power 1.0 mW — at a screen. She sets out to find the energy carried by each photon, in joules and in electron-volts, and then how many photons stream out of the laser every second.
greene.7: Substitution (electron-volts): E = (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 3.0 × 10⁸) ÷ (5.32 × 10⁻⁷) = 2.3 eV — the very same energy, just dressed in different units, since 3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹ ÷ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.3.
greene.8: Answer: each photon carries 2.3 eV. The laser pours out 1.0 × 10⁻³ J of energy every second, so to find how many photons that takes we simply share the total among the individual parcels: n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵.
greene.9: Key idea The energy of a single photon is fixed by its frequency, and by nothing else: E = hf. Intensity governs only how many photons arrive each second — never how much energy any one of them carries.
greene.10: The photoelectric effect is the ejection of electrons from a metal surface when light of sufficiently high frequency falls upon it. greene.11: Make the collector positive and every ejected electron is drawn obediently across the gap; the photocurrent then saturates, meaning that pushing the collector still more positive gathers no extra current — there are simply no more electrons left to gather. greene.13: Work the problem in electron-volts and the conversion melts away entirely: a stopping voltage of 1.40 V means Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Figure 8.2 traces the full photocurrent–voltage curve for the same light at two intensities.
greene.14: Einstein made sense of all this with the photon model, and the picture is beautifully simple. Each photon is swallowed whole by a single electron — never split between two — and the most loosely held electrons need some minimum energy φ, the work function of that particular metal, just to break free of the surface. greene.15: Electrons torn from deeper inside the metal pay extra tolls on their way out, so they emerge carrying less than Ek max. Set Ek max = 0 — the bare threshold of escape — and you find the threshold frequency f0, the lowest frequency that can pry any electron at all from that metal:
greene.16: Plot Ek max against f and Einstein’s equation reveals itself as a straight line, drawn in Figure 8.3. Lay Ek max = hf − φ alongside y = mx + c and the graph reads itself: the gradient is h — the same for every metal in the universe — the horizontal intercept is f0, and the line, run backwards, meets the vertical axis at −φ. greene.17: In the photon picture, the intensity of monochromatic light is nothing more than the number of photons arriving each second, every one of them carrying hf. greene.23: Connects The stopping-voltage measurement is simply W = qV from electric fields running in reverse — here the field strips kinetic energy away rather than feeding it in — and the electron-volt first defined there is the working currency of this entire area of study.
greene.24: By 1905 the wave model of light had a full century of interference evidence at its back. Turn it loose on the photoelectric effect, though, and it makes three confident predictions — and watch each one fail in turn.

greene.27: Connects Here we watch a model collide with evidence it cannot explain away. The mirror image waits on the next page, where electron diffraction turns the same trick against the particle model of matter.

bronowski.0: For a hundred years the patient evidence of interference had spoken with one voice, and that voice said: light is a wave. But there was a single experiment the wave could not answer for — and it was decisive. Light, it turns out, delivers its energy not in a continuous stream but in indivisible parcels we call photons; and the photoelectric effect is the instrument that counts them, one by one.
bronowski.1: Light hands over its energy in indivisible parcels, and we call them photons. A photon of frequency f carries one fixed measure of energy, set in strict proportion to that frequency — it cannot surrender half of itself, and a surface cannot quietly hoard it grain by grain until enough has gathered. The constant that ties energy to frequency is Planck’s constant h:
bronowski.2: Measured in joules the energy of a single photon is so small as to be unwieldy, and so throughout this area of study we reach instead for the electron-volt: the energy one electron gains as it is carried through a potential difference of 1 V. Apply W = qV with q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, and the bridge between the two units stands before us:
bronowski.3: The formula sheet sets down Planck’s constant in both currencies at once. Take h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s when the answer is wanted in electron-volts, and h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s when it is wanted in joules — to choose the right value at the outset is to spare yourself a conversion at the end.
bronowski.4: Anika turns a green laser pointer of wavelength 532 nm and output power 1.0 mW upon a screen. She sets out to find the energy each photon carries, in joules and in electron-volts, and how many photons stream from the laser in a single second.
bronowski.7: Substitution (electron-volts): E = (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 3.0 × 10⁸) ÷ (5.32 × 10⁻⁷) = 2.3 eV — one and the same energy, for 3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹ ÷ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.3.
bronowski.8: Answer: every photon carries 2.3 eV. The laser pours out 1.0 × 10⁻³ J each second, so the number of photons leaving in that second is n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵.
bronowski.9: Key idea The energy of each photon is fixed by frequency, and by frequency alone: E = hf. Intensity decides only how many photons arrive in a second — never how much energy any single one of them brings.
bronowski.10: The photoelectric effect is the casting-out of electrons from the surface of a metal struck by light of high enough frequency. The apparatus drawn in Figure 8.1 is the instrument that measures it: monochromatic light enters an evacuated tube and falls upon a metal plate, the photoelectrons so freed cross the gap to a collector electrode, and the photocurrent that results is read upon an ammeter. A variable DC supply fixes the potential difference between the electrodes, and we are free to reverse its polarity at will.
bronowski.11: With the collector held positive, every freed electron is drawn across the gap, and the photocurrent saturates: to make the collector still more positive gathers not one electron more, for every photoelectron is already gathered in. Make the collector negative, and now each electron crossing the gap must yield up kinetic energy W = qV to the field that opposes it; so as the reverse voltage mounts, the slowest electrons are the first to be turned back, and the current falls away.
bronowski.12: At the stopping voltage V0 even the swiftest photoelectrons are halted before they can reach the collector, and the photocurrent sinks to zero. The stopping voltage thus becomes a measure of the greatest kinetic energy the photoelectrons can possess:
bronowski.13: Worked in electron-volts the conversion vanishes of its own accord: a stopping voltage of 1.40 V tells us at once that Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Figure 8.2 sets out the full photocurrent–voltage curve for the same light at two intensities.
bronowski.14: It was Einstein who read these measurements aright, through the photon model. Each photon is swallowed whole by a single electron — no electron takes two — and the most loosely held electrons demand a least energy φ, the work function of that metal, before they can break free of the surface. Whatever remains, a surface electron keeps for its own:
bronowski.15: Electrons torn from deeper within the metal squander energy on their journey out, and so emerge with less than Ek max. Set Ek max = 0 and you arrive at the threshold frequency f0, the least frequency that can prise any electron at all from that metal:
bronowski.16: Plot Ek max against f and Einstein’s equation reveals itself as a straight line, drawn in Figure 8.3. Set Ek max = hf − φ beside y = mx + c and the graph yields its secrets directly: the gradient is h — the very same for every metal there is — the horizontal intercept is f0, and the line carried back meets the vertical axis at −φ. Different metals give lines that run parallel: change the metal and you shift both intercepts, but the gradient you can never touch.
bronowski.17: In the photon model the intensity of monochromatic light is nothing but the number of photons arriving each second, each one bearing its hf. But raise the frequency while holding the light’s power steady, and each photon now carries more, so that fewer arrive each second, and the saturation current falls.
bronowski.18: Trap Brighter is never faster. To change the intensity changes the photocurrent and nothing else; Ek max and the stopping voltage are settled by the frequency and the work function alone. And light beneath the threshold frequency loosens nothing whatever, however fierce its brightness and however long it is left to shine.

bronowski.20: Formula: Ek max = hf − φ, with h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s, since the data come to us in electron-volts.
bronowski.22: Answer: Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, and so the stopping voltage is V0 = 1.0 V. The threshold frequency of caesium is f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz, so the violet light lies above the threshold.
bronowski.23: Connects The stopping-voltage measurement is W = qV from electric fields run backwards — here the field strips kinetic energy away instead of bestowing it — and the electron-volt first defined there is the working coin of this entire area of study.
bronowski.24: By 1905 the wave model of light carried behind it a full century of interference evidence. Turn it upon the photoelectric effect and it makes three plain predictions — and not one of the three survives the test.

bronowski.27: Connects Here a model is brought face to face with evidence it cannot account for; the mirror-image case waits on the next page, where electron diffraction rises up to contradict the particle model of matter.

Hold the frequency fixed above f0 and double the intensity, and you double the number of photoelectrons freed each second, and so double the saturation current — yet each electron is still set loose by a single photon of the same energy, so Ek max and V0 are left untouched, exactly as Figure 8.2 reveals. But raise the frequency while holding the light power constant, and now each photon carries more energy, so fewer photons arrive each second, and the saturation current falls.
attenborough.18: Trap Brighter never means faster. Alter the intensity and you alter the photocurrent alone; Ek max and the stopping voltage are set by the frequency and the work function. And light below the threshold frequency frees nothing whatsoever — however fierce its glare, and however long it shines.

attenborough.20: Formula: Ek max = hf − φ, with h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s, since the data are given in electron-volts.
attenborough.22: Answer: Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, and so the stopping voltage is V0 = 1.0 V. The threshold frequency of caesium is f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz, and so the violet light lies above the threshold frequency.
attenborough.23: Connects The stopping-voltage measurement is simply W = qV from electric fields run backwards — here the field strips kinetic energy away rather than bestowing it — and the electron-volt, first defined there, is the working currency of this entire area of study.
attenborough.24: By 1905 the wave model of light stood upon a century of interference evidence. Yet set it to the task of the photoelectric effect, and it makes three clear predictions — and every one of them, in its turn, fails.
attenborough.25: The photon model accounts for all three observations in a single stroke. Energy arrives in indivisible packets of hf; one photon frees one electron; and the transfer is instant: if hf < φ the electron cannot escape and there is nothing to be gathered up, while if hf > φ the very first photons absorbed cast out electrons without the slightest delay. Frequency governs the energy of each packet; intensity governs only how many packets arrive each second.
attenborough.26: Key idea The photoelectric effect is the evidence that light is quantised. The wave model fails on intensity, on threshold frequency and on time delay; the photon model explains all three with E = hf.
attenborough.27: Connects Here a model comes face to face with evidence it cannot explain away; the mirror image awaits on the next page, where electron diffraction rises up to contradict the particle model of matter.

greekepic.0: For a hundred years the testimony of interference upheld the wave as the true nature of light. Yet one trial would not bow to that creed: light pours out its energy not as a flowing stream but in indivisible parcels, called photons, and the photoelectric effect is the rite by which mortals measure them.
greekepic.1: Light bestows its energy in indivisible parcels named photons. A photon of frequency f bears one fixed measure of energy, woven in proportion to that frequency — it cannot surrender half its gift, nor may a surface gather it grain by grain. Planck’s constant h stands as the constant of that proportion:
greekepic.2: Photon energies reckoned in joules are vexingly small, and so this study labours chiefly in the electron-volt: the energy won by a single electron borne through a potential difference of 1 V. Wielding W = qV with q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C yields the conversion:
greekepic.3: The formula sheet inscribes Planck’s constant in both systems of measure. Take up h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s when the energy is sought in electron-volts, and h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s when it is sought in joules — for to choose rightly is to cast away a step of conversion.
greekepic.4: Anika levels a green laser pointer, of wavelength 532 nm and output power 1.0 mW, upon a screen. She reckons the energy of each photon, in joules and in electron-volts, and the number of photons that depart the laser with every passing second.
greekepic.7: Substitution (electron-volts): E = (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 3.0 × 10⁸) ÷ (5.32 × 10⁻⁷) = 2.3 eV — the selfsame energy, for 3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹ ÷ 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.3.
greekepic.8: Answer: each photon carries 2.3 eV. The laser bestows 1.0 × 10⁻³ J of energy with every second, and so the number of photons sent forth each second is n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵.
greekepic.9: Key idea The energy of each photon is decreed by frequency alone: E = hf. Intensity rules only how many photons arrive in a second — never how great a portion of energy each one bears.
greekepic.10: The photoelectric effect is the casting-out of electrons from a metal surface bathed in light of frequency high enough. The apparatus drawn in Figure 8.1 measures it: monochromatic light enters an evacuated tube and falls upon a metal plate, the banished photoelectrons cross the gulf to a collector electrode, and the photocurrent thus raised is read upon an ammeter. A variable DC supply ordains the potential difference between the electrodes, and its polarity may be reversed at will.
greekepic.11: With the collector made positive, every banished electron is drawn across the gulf and the photocurrent saturates: to make the collector more positive still gathers no further current, for every photoelectron is already gathered in. With the collector made negative, each crossing electron must yield up kinetic energy W = qV to the opposing field, so that as the reverse voltage swells, the slower electrons are turned back first and the current wanes.
greekepic.12: At the stopping voltage V0 even the swiftest photoelectrons are brought to rest ere they reach the collector, and the photocurrent falls to nothing. The stopping voltage therefore takes the measure of the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons:
greekepic.13: Worked in electron-volts the conversion vanishes away: a stopping voltage of 1.40 V proclaims Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Figure 8.2 reveals the full photocurrent–voltage curve for the same light at two intensities.
greekepic.14: Einstein laid bare these measurements by the decree of the photon. Each photon is swallowed whole by a single electron — no electron drinks of two — and the most loosely held electrons demand a least measure of energy φ, the work function of that metal, to win free of the surface. An electron at the surface keeps whatever remains:
greekepic.15: Electrons cast out from deeper within the metal squander further energy on their passage outward, and so they emerge bearing less than Ek max. Setting Ek max = 0 yields the threshold frequency f0, the least frequency that can cast out any electron from that metal at all:
greekepic.16: To plot Ek max against f is to turn Einstein’s equation into a straight-line graph, shown in Figure 8.3. greekepic.17: In the decree of the photon, the intensity of monochromatic light is ordained by the number of photons arriving each second, each bearing hf. At a fixed frequency above f0, to double the intensity is to double the number of photoelectrons cast out each second, and therefore to double the saturation current — yet each electron is still banished by a single photon of the selfsame energy, so Ek max and V0 stand unchanged, even as Figure 8.2 reveals. greekepic.18: Trap Brighter is never swifter. To change the intensity changes the photocurrent and nothing more; Ek max and the stopping voltage are decreed by the frequency and the work function. And likewise, light beneath the threshold frequency casts out nothing at all, however fierce its blaze and however long it shines.

greekepic.22: Answer: Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, and so the stopping voltage is V0 = 1.0 V. The threshold frequency of caesium is f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz, and so the violet light rides above the threshold frequency.
greekepic.23: Connects The stopping-voltage measurement is W = qV from electric fields run backward — the field strips kinetic energy away instead of bestowing it — and the electron-volt named there is the coin of the realm throughout this whole area of study.
greekepic.24: By 1905 the wave model of light had a century of interference evidence at its back. Set against the photoelectric effect, it utters three clear prophecies — and every one of them falls to ruin.

cnpop.0: 整整一个世纪的干涉实验都在给光的波动模型站台。可偏偏有一个实验唱反调：光把能量一份一份地交出去，每一份都是不可再分的小包裹，叫做光子；而光电效应，就是专门把它们逮个正着的那场实验。
cnpop.1: 光传递能量的方式很轴：一份一份打包好，每一份都是不可再分的小包裹，叫做光子。一个频率为 f 的光子，扛着一份固定不变的能量，跟频率成正比——它不能只交一半能量出来，金属表面也别想慢慢攒。普朗克常数 h 就是那个比例系数：
cnpop.2: 用焦耳来量光子能量，那数字小得离谱，用着实在别扭，所以这一块我们基本都改用电子伏特来开工：一个电子被 1 V 的电势差加速一遍，所获得的那点能量。把 W = qV 搬出来，代入 q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C，换算关系当场出炉：
cnpop.3: 公式表很贴心，普朗克常数两套单位都给你印好了。要算能量是电子伏特，就用 h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s；要算焦耳，就用 h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s——一开始挑对那个值，等于把一整步换算直接省掉，舒服。
cnpop.4: Anika 拿一支波长 532 nm、输出功率 1.0 mW 的绿色激光笔，对着屏幕一照。她要算出每个光子的能量，焦耳和电子伏特都来一份，再算出激光每秒钟射出多少个光子。
cnpop.8: 答案：每个光子扛着 2.3 eV。激光每秒钟送出 1.0 × 10⁻³ J 的能量，所以每秒射出的光子数就是 n = (1.0 × 10⁻³) ÷ (3.74 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 2.7 × 10¹⁵ 个。
cnpop.9: Key idea 每个光子的能量，光看频率就拍板了：E = hf。光强只管每秒钟来多少个光子——它永远说了不算每个光子扛多少能量。
cnpop.10: 光电效应说白了就是：拿频率够高的光去照金属表面，电子就被一脚踹出来。图 8.1 里那套装置专门测它：单色光钻进一根抽成真空的管子，砸到金属板上，被踹出来的光电子飞越间隙冲向收集电极，由此产生的光电流由电流计读出来。一个可调直流电源负责设定两个电极之间的电势差，而且极性还能反着接。
cnpop.11: 收集极接正的时候，每个被踹出来的电子都被乖乖拉过间隙，光电流就饱和了：你把收集极搞得再正，也收不到一丁点多余电流，因为光电子早就被一网打尽了。换成收集极接负，每个想穿过去的电子都得交出动能 W = qV 去对抗那个反向场，所以反向电压越拉越大，跑得慢的电子先被劝退，电流跟着往下掉。
cnpop.12: 到了截止电压 V0，连跑得最快的那批光电子都还没摸到收集极就被刹停了，光电流直接归零。所以截止电压恰好量出了光电子的最大动能：
cnpop.13: 用电子伏特来算，换算这一步直接蒸发：截止电压 1.40 V 就意味着 Ek max = 1.40 eV = 2.24 × 10⁻¹⁹ J。图 8.2 给出了同一束光在两种光强下完整的光电流–电压曲线。
cnpop.14: 爱因斯坦用光子模型把这些测量结果一锅端了。每个光子被单个电子整个吞下——没有哪个电子能吃两个——而那些绑得最松的电子，想逃出表面至少得攒够一份能量 φ，也就是这块金属的功函数。表面电子吃完功函数，剩下多少就揣着多少走人：
cnpop.15: 从金属深处被踹出来的电子，往外冲的路上还得再赔掉一些能量，所以它们出来时能量比 Ek max 还低。令 Ek max = 0，就解出了阈频率 f0，也就是能从这块金属上踹出任何一个电子的最低频率：
cnpop.16: 把 Ek max 对 f 一画，爱因斯坦那个方程当场变成一条直线，见图 8.3。拿 Ek max = hf − φ 跟 y = mx + c 一对照，这图就能直接读：斜率是 h——对每种金属都一个样——横轴截距是 f0，把直线往回延长，撞上纵轴的地方就是 −φ。换不同金属画出来的是一堆平行线：换金属能挪动两个截距，可斜率你怎么折腾都纹丝不动。
cnpop.17: 在光子模型里，单色光的光强就是每秒钟来多少个光子说了算，每个光子扛着 hf。在某个高于 f0 的固定频率上，光强翻一倍，每秒被踹出来的光电子就翻一倍，饱和电流也跟着翻一倍——可每个电子照样是被一个同样能量的光子单挑踹出来的，所以 Ek max 和 V0 纹丝不动，正如图 8.2 演的那样。但要是反过来，功率不变只把频率往上抬，每个光子能量变大，那每秒来的光子数反而变少了，饱和电流就往下掉。
cnpop.18: Trap 别脑补：更亮从来不等于更快。改光强只动光电流这一项；Ek max 和截止电压是由频率和功函数定死的。同理，频率低于阈频率的光，啥都踹不出来——你照得再亮、照得再久，都白搭。
cnpop.19: Kenji 拿频率 7.5 × 10¹⁴ Hz 的紫光去照光电池上那层铯表面。铯的功函数是 φ = 2.1 eV。
cnpop.20: 公式：Ek max = hf − φ，这里用 h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s，因为数据都是电子伏特。
cnpop.22: 答案：Ek max = 1.0 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J，所以截止电压就是 V0 = 1.0 V。铯的阈频率 f0 = φ/h = 2.1 ÷ (4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵) = 5.1 × 10¹⁴ Hz，所以这束紫光稳稳压过了阈频率。
cnpop.23: Connects 这个测截止电压的操作，本质就是电场那章的 W = qV 倒着跑一遍——场不是给电子喂能量，而是反过来把动能抽走——而那章定义的电子伏特，就是这一整块内容通用的硬通货。
cnpop.24: 到 1905 年，光的波动模型背后已经压着整整一个世纪的干涉证据。可一把它套到光电效应上，它给出三条干脆利落的预言——然后一条不落，全军覆没。
cnpop.25: 光子模型一出手，三个观测一次性全摆平。能量是一份一份不可再分的 hf 包裹送过来的，一个光子踹出一个电子，而且这交接是当场完成、毫不拖泥带水：要是 hf < φ，电子根本逃不出去，也压根没有东西可攒；要是 hf > φ，那第一批被吸收的光子就立刻把电子踹出来，毫不墨迹。频率管每个包裹装多少能量；光强只管每秒钟送来多少个包裹。
cnpop.26: Key idea 光电效应就是光被量子化的铁证。波动模型在光强、阈频率和时间延迟这三条上全翻车；光子模型靠一个 E = hf 把三条全讲明白了。
cnpop.27: Connects 这里是一个模型撞上了它怎么都圆不回来的证据；反过来的剧情就在下一页，那里电子衍射专门来打脸物质的粒子模型。
